key_concepts: Microeconomics: Study of individual agents (consumers, firms, workers) and their behaviour
Macroeconomics: Study of aggregate economic activity, income, employment, price level
Consumer surplus: Difference between price willing to pay vs actual price paid
Producer surplus: Difference between price received vs minimum price willing to accept
Pareto Efficiency: Condition where no one can be made better off without making someone worse off
Ceteris Paribus: 'All other things equal' assumption in economic analysis
Opportunity cost: Cost of foregone alternative when choosing one option
Law of diminishing returns: Marginal product declines as more variable input is added
Economies of scale: Long-run average costs fall as firm expands output
Diseconomies of scale: Long-run average costs rise as firm expands output
Externalities: Costs/benefits affecting third parties not reflected in market price
Positive externality: Benefits spill over to third parties (education, vaccination)
Negative externality: Costs imposed on third parties (pollution, noise)
Public goods: Non-excludable, non-rivalrous (national defence, lighthouse)
topics.current_affairs_connect: Understanding micro-macro linkages essential for analyzing GST impact, inflation dynamics, employment data releases

key_concepts: Demand: Quantity of goods/services consumers willing and able to buy at various prices
Law of Demand: Inverse relationship between price and quantity demanded (ceteris paribus)
Demand curve shifts: Change in non-price determinants (income, preferences, substitute prices)
Supply: Quantity of goods/services producers willing to sell at various prices
Law of Supply: Positive relationship between price and quantity supplied
Supply curve shifts: Changes in production costs, technology, taxes, subsidies
Market equilibrium: Point where quantity demanded equals quantity supplied
Price elasticity of demand (PED): Responsiveness of quantity demanded to price change; PED = % ΔQd / % ΔP
Classification of PED: Elastic (|PED| > 1), Inelastic (|PED| < 1), Unit elastic (|PED| = 1)
Income elasticity of demand: Responsiveness to income changes; normal goods (positive), inferior goods (negative)
Cross elasticity of demand: Responsiveness to price changes of related goods; substitutes (positive), complements (negative)
Price elasticity of supply: Responsiveness of quantity supplied to price changes; PES = % ΔQs / % ΔP
Determinants of PED: Availability of substitutes, necessity vs luxury, proportion of income spent, time period
Deadweight loss: Loss of economic efficiency when supply/demand not in equilibrium; occurs due to taxes, price controls
topics.current_affairs_connect: Agricultural price support (MSP) related to supply response; food inflation and demand-supply dynamics; fuel prices and elasticity impact on consumer behaviour

key_concepts: Perfect Competition: Many firms, homogeneous products, free entry/exit, perfect information; P = AR = MR at equilibrium
Short-run equilibrium in perfect competition: Firm produces where MR = MC; may earn supernormal, normal, or negative profits
Long-run equilibrium in perfect competition: Free entry drives supernormal profits to zero; all firms earn normal profit
Monopoly: Single firm, unique product, high barriers to entry; creates deadweight loss by restricting output
Monopoly power sources: Natural monopoly (declining AC), patent/copyright, government licence, control of key input
Monopolistic Competition: Many firms, differentiated products, easy entry/exit; firms have some price-setting power
Oligopoly: Few large firms dominate, homogeneous/differentiated products, barriers to entry; interdependence of firms
Oligopoly examples in India: Telecom (Jio, Airtel, Vodafone), cement (Ambuja, Ultratech), aviation (IndiGo, Spicejet)
Cartel: Group of firms collude on price/output; OPEC example; often unstable due to cheating incentives
Monopolistic competition equilibrium: P > AR = MR; firms earn zero economic profit in long run; excess capacity remains
Barriers to entry: Economies of scale, brand loyalty, switching costs, network effects, regulatory barriers
Market concentration measures: Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI), concentration ratio (CR4, CR8)
Competition policy in India: Competition Act 2002, CCI (Competition Commission of India), focus on anti-competitive practices
Predatory pricing, exclusive dealing, tie-in sales: Anticompetitive practices regulated by CCI
topics.current_affairs_connect: CCI investigations into Big Tech companies, competition in telecom/pharma sectors; cab aggregators (Uber/Ola) regulations; FDI in retail and impact on competition

key_concepts: Gross Domestic Product (GDP): Total monetary value of all final goods/services produced within domestic territory in one year
GDP = C + I + G + (X - M) where C=consumption, I=investment, G=government spending, X-M=net exports
Gross National Product (GNP): GDP + Net Factor Income from Abroad (NFIA); measures income earned by nationals
GNP = GDP + NFIA; NFIA = Income of nationals from abroad - Income of foreigners from domestic territory
Net National Product (NNP): GNP - Depreciation (consumption of fixed capital); represents sustainable income
NNP = GNP - CFC (Capital Consumption); also called National Income at factor cost
Net Domestic Product (NDP): GDP - Depreciation; NDP = GDP - CFC
Gross Value Added (GVA): Difference between total output and intermediate consumption; GVA = GDP - product taxes + subsidies
GVA at factor cost vs GVA at basic prices vs GVA at market prices: Different treatment of taxes and subsidies
Per Capita Income: Total National Income / Total Population; adjusted for inflation gives Real Per Capita Income
Purchasing Power Parity (PPP): Exchange rate adjusted for price level differences between countries; better reflects living standards
GDP at constant prices (Real GDP): Adjusted for inflation using base year prices; shows true growth
GDP at current prices (Nominal GDP): Measured at prices of the year; inflated by price increases
GDP Deflator: Ratio of Nominal GDP to Real GDP × 100; measures inflation in GDP
topics.current_affairs_connect: India's recent GDP growth revisions (2021-22 data); sectoral GVA contributions (agriculture vs services); impact of inflation on real GDP; comparison with other BRICS nations

topics.topic_name: Real vs Nominal GDP & Per Capita Income

ncert_reference.chapter_name: Introduction to Macroeconomics and its Concepts

key_concepts: Real GDP (Constant Price): Adjusted for inflation; shows growth in actual output; comparable across time periods
Nominal GDP (Current Price): Measured at prices of current year; includes inflation effect
Real GDP = Nominal GDP / GDP Deflator × 100; more accurate reflection of economic growth
GDP deflator: Weighted average of price changes across all goods/services in economy
Base year: Reference year used for calculating real GDP; India uses 2011-12 as base year for current series
Example: If nominal GDP grows 10% but inflation is 5%, real GDP growth = approx 4.76%
Per Capita Income (PCI): Total National Income / Total Population; indicates average income per person
India's PCI in nominal terms: Approximately 187,000-192,000 INR (2024-25); in USD terms approx 2,500-2,700
Purchasing Power Parity (PPP) adjusted PCI: Accounts for price differences; India's PPP-PCI much higher than nominal
PPP adjustment: Indian rupee undervalued vs USD; prices lower in India for same basket of goods
World Bank methodology: PPP conversion factors used to make cross-country income comparisons meaningful
India's global ranking: 5th largest economy nominal GDP, 3rd largest by PPP GDP (post-2023)
Sectoral GDP contribution: Agriculture 18%, Industry 24%, Services 58% (2023-24 approx); changing composition
Structural change: Shift from agricultural to service economy as development occurs
topics.current_affairs_connect: India reaching $3 trillion nominal GDP milestone (March 2024); growth rate comparisons with other economies; impact of inflation on real growth numbers; sectoral shifts post-pandemic

key_concepts: Capitalist economy: Private ownership, market forces determine allocation, profit motive, competition, minimal state intervention
Socialist economy: State ownership, central planning, equitable distribution, no profit motive, elimination of class differences
Mixed economy: Combination of private and public sector, both market and planning, India's system (Article 39 of Constitution)
Command economy: Central authority decides production, distribution; examples: North Korea, former USSR
Market economy: Price mechanism allocates resources; consumer choices drive production; examples: USA, UK (historically)
Primary sector: Agriculture, forestry, fishing, mining; extraction of natural resources; 18% of Indian GDP, 42% employment
Secondary sector: Manufacturing, construction, utilities; processing raw materials; 24% of Indian GDP, 24% employment
Tertiary sector: Services - trade, transport, communication, finance, education, health; 58% of Indian GDP, 34% employment
Quaternary sector: Knowledge-based services - IT, research, education; growing importance in modern economies
Sectoral interdependence: Backward linkage (primary to secondary), forward linkage (secondary to tertiary)
Structural change in India: Decline in agriculture's share, rise in services (tertiary-led growth); incomplete industrialization
India's structural composition: Service-led growth model (vs East Asian manufacturing-led); FDI mainly in services
Informal vs formal sectors: Informal sector 90% employment (agriculture, unregistered); formal sector 10% (organized)
Transition dynamics: Young population provides demographic dividend; need for job creation in formal sector
topics.current_affairs_connect: India's sectoral composition changes post-pandemic; formalization of informal sector (E-Shram portal); service sector dominance debate; agriculture distress and structural unemployment

key_concepts: Absolute poverty: Inability to meet basic needs (food, shelter, clothing, health, education); fixed standard
Relative poverty: Having less compared to others in society; depends on societal income distribution
Poverty line: Minimum expenditure required to meet basic needs; varies by committee estimates
Lakdawala Committee (1993): Used calorie-based approach; set poverty line at Rs. 328 (urban), Rs. 231 (rural) per capita/month
Tendulkar Committee (2009): Adopted Mixed Reference Period (MRP) approach; raised poverty line significantly
Tendulkar line estimates (2009-10): Rs. 672 (urban), Rs. 446 (rural) per month; estimated 32% population below line
Rangarajan Committee (2014): Further raised poverty line (most liberal estimate); Rs. 1,000 (urban), Rs. 816 (rural) for 2011-12
Rangarajan line (2011-12): Poverty at 41% national level; called poverty 'rediscovery'; faced criticism for being too generous
Multidimensional Poverty Index (MPI): Measures deprivation across multiple dimensions (health, education, living standards)
MPI dimensions: Education, health, living standards; 10 indicators total; NITI Aayog calculates for India
Below Poverty Line (BPL) vs Above Poverty Line (APL): Census data tracks BPL households; used for targeted welfare
SECC 2011 (Socio-Economic Caste Census): Basis for BPL determination; identifies excluded/included households; not yet fully released
Headcount ratio: % population below poverty line; declines from ~45% (1993-94) to ~22% (2019-20)
Poverty gap: Measures intensity of poverty (depth); poverty severity index measures inequality among poor
topics.current_affairs_connect: SECC 2011 delayed release and controversies; NITI Aayog's MPI reports; poverty reduction targets under SDGs; pandemic impact on poverty; state-wise variations in poverty rates

key_concepts: Inequality: Unequal distribution of income/wealth across population; measured using various indices
Gini Coefficient: Ranges 0-1 (or 0-100%); 0 = perfect equality, 1 = perfect inequality
Gini formula: G = Σ(2i - n - 1)×yi / (n × Σyi) where i=rank, yi=income; higher Gini = more inequality
Lorenz Curve: Cumulative income distribution curve; 45° diagonal = perfect equality; curve below diagonal = inequality
Lorenz curve interpretation: Distance from diagonal = extent of inequality; convexity shows income concentration
Palma Ratio: Richest 10% income / poorest 40% income; newer measure gaining prominence
Decile share analysis: Income share of top 10%, next 10%, etc.; shows concentration patterns
Quintile analysis: 5 income groups; common in policy analysis (e.g., richest quintile's income share)
India's inequality: Gini coefficient approx 0.31-0.35 (2019-20 NFHS); rising trend post-liberalization
Rural vs urban inequality: Urban areas show higher inequality (Gini ~0.38) vs rural (~0.30)
Inter-state inequality: Significant variations; wealthier states (Delhi, Maharashtra) vs poor states (Bihar, Odisha)
Wealth inequality: More severe than income inequality; top 10% owns 65-70% wealth
Intergenerational mobility: Poor to rich transition probability in India: ~15-20%; sticky poverty
Consumption inequality vs income inequality: Consumption smoother; consumption inequality < income inequality
topics.current_affairs_connect: Rising inequality post-pandemic; WEF Global Inequality Report; wealth concentration among top 1%; gender wealth gap; caste-based economic disparities; inequality in access to digital resources

key_concepts: Unemployment: Percentage of labour force (willing and able to work) unable to find jobs despite searching
Labour force: Working age population (15-59 years typically) able and willing to work; includes employed + unemployed
Unemployment rate (U-rate): Unemployed / Labour force × 100; formula: U = U / (E + U) × 100
Frictional unemployment: Short-term, due to job transition; inevitable in dynamic economy; normal friction
Structural unemployment: Long-term, mismatch between skills required and available; technological change impact
Cyclical unemployment: Due to economic downturns; occurs during recessions; inverse to business cycle
Seasonal unemployment: Due to seasonal demand variations; agriculture, tourism sectors; temporary nature
Disguised unemployment: Person employed but productivity very low; redundant worker; common in agriculture
Open unemployment: Person actively seeking work but unable to find; visible unemployment
NSSO (National Sample Survey Organization): Conducts PLFS (Periodic Labour Force Survey); measures unemployment; quarterly data
PLFS methodology: Sample of 45,000+ households; definition: without work, seeking work, available for work in past week
Natural rate of unemployment: Unemployment when economy at full capacity; structural + frictional; India's approx 4-5%
Youth unemployment: Higher than overall; problem for 15-24 age group in India ~12-15% (PLFS data)
Graduate unemployment: Educated unemployment rising; mismatch between education and job requirements
topics.current_affairs_connect: India's unemployment rising to 6.5%+ post-pandemic; PLFS quarterly releases showing trends; youth unemployment crisis; skill gap in IT sector vs other sectors; impact of automation on employment

ncert_reference.chapter_name: Employment: Growth, Informalisation and Other Issues

key_concepts: MGNREGA (Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Act 2005): Right to 100 days paid work/year; launched nationally 2008
MGNREGA objectives: Provide livelihood security, generate productive assets, social protection, women empowerment
MGNREGA wage: Linked to state minimum wages; approx Rs. 300-400/day in most states (2024); 33% must be women
MGNREGA employment: FY 2023-24: 300+ crore person-days; approx 80 lakh households; budget allocation ~Rs. 74,000 crore
MGNREGA work types: Road construction, water conservation, afforestation, land development, rural sanitation
MGNREGA outcomes: Created 5000+ km roads, 3 crore+ hectares treated land, 2 lakh+ water harvesting structures
MGNREGA issues: Delay in wage payment, low asset quality, low female participation (despite 33% mandate), displacement
Criticism: Opportunity cost (migration to cities better), inefficient asset creation, political misuse
Labour Code 2020: Consolidated 29 old laws into 4 codes (wages, IR, social security, occupational safety)
Code on Wages 2019: Defines minimum wage, wages structure, gratuity; floor to wages ensuring living standards
Industrial Relations Code 2020: Regulates employer-employee relations, strikes, dispute resolution; controversial provisions
Occupational Safety Code 2020: Covers occupational health, safety standards, workplace hazards
Social Security Code 2020: Covers workers' compensation, disability, unemployment benefits, maternity, old age pensions
Labour reforms debate: Ease of hiring/firing vs worker protection; state-wise variations in implementation
topics.current_affairs_connect: MGNREGA spike during pandemic/lockdowns; asset quality debates; farm labour distress; labour code implementation challenges; debate on universal basic income as alternative; migrant worker issues

topics.topic_name: Economic Planning in India & Five Year Plans
key_concepts: Economic planning: Centralized decision-making on resource allocation toward development goals
Planning Commission (1950-2014): Established by Dr. Rajendra Prasad; Jawaharlal Nehru as Chairman; drafted FYPs
Five Year Plans: Periodical 5-year development targets; 15 FYPs completed (1951-2016); now abandoned
1st FYP (1951-56): Mahalanobis model focus; agriculture priority; capital accumulation; growth rate 3.2%
2nd FYP (1956-61): Heavy industry focus; steel plants, dams; foundation of industrial base; 4.2% growth
3rd FYP (1961-66): Self-reliance goal; agricultural focus; wars disrupted; 2.8% growth (lowest)
4th FYP (1969-74): Poverty alleviation focus; green revolution impact; growth 5.7% (high); inflation crisis ended
5th FYP (1974-78): Equity focus; employment emphasis; implemented by JP-led government in 1977; growth 4.3%
6th FYP (1980-85): Growth & equity; technology emphasis; economic stabilization; growth 5.7%
7th FYP (1985-90): Human development focus; liberalization hints; growth 6.8% (fast growth period)
8th FYP (1992-97): Post-LPG reforms; integration into world economy; growth 6.5%; structural changes
9th FYP (1997-02): Social development priority; education, health; growth 5.5%; poverty focus
10th FYP (2002-07): Inclusive growth concept; infrastructure investment; growth 7.7%; fast growth begins
11th FYP (2007-12): Inclusive growth; social sectors; agriculture & rural development; growth 7.9%
12th FYP (2012-16): Inclusive, sustainable, faster growth; skill development; growth 6.7% (slowdown period)
Mahalanobis model: Capital-intensive heavy industry focus; capital accumulation path; influenced 2nd-4th FYPs
topics.current_affairs_connect: NITI Aayog replaced Planning Commission (2015); shift from planning to aspirational India; centralized planning waning; revival of plan/strategy discussion; long-term vision documents (2047 agenda)

ncert_reference.chapter_name: The Responsibility of Government and Market

key_concepts: NITI Aayog (National Institution for Transforming India): Established 2015; replaces Planning Commission
NITI structure: PM as Chairperson, CM of states as members, sector experts, special invitees; think-tank model
NITI functions: Advising government on policy, research, cooperative federalism, SDG alignment, aspirational districts
Difference from Planning Commission: Non-binding recommendations vs binding allocations; advisory vs directive role
LPG Reforms 1991: Liberalization, Privatization, Globalization; triggered by BoP crisis, high inflation
1991 crisis: Foreign exchange crisis, inflation 13%, external debt crisis; IMF intervention
Liberalization: Removal of license raj, reduction in controls, ease of doing business, increased competition
Privatization: Private sector entry in previously reserved sectors (airlines, telecom, ports, insurance)
Globalization: Integration into world economy, FDI inflows, export promotion, reduced tariffs, capital account opening
Delicensing: Removal of industrial licensing requirement; except defence, nuclear, hazardous chemicals
Automatic route FDI: FDI up to certain % allowed automatically without government approval in many sectors
Reduction in tariffs: Effective rate from 80-90% (pre-1991) to 11% (2020s); WTO compliance
Capital account convertibility: Partial convertibility achieved 1994; full convertibility still pending (restrictions remain)
Washington Consensus: Market-oriented reforms pushed by IMF/World Bank; criticized for social impact
Post-Washington Consensus: Greater emphasis on social protection, inequality, environmental sustainability; evolved approach
topics.current_affairs_connect: Atmanirbhar Bharat vs globalization debate; Make in India vs imports; RCEP rejection; sector-wise FDI trends; recent foreign policy economic dimension; new industrial policy frameworks

key_concepts: Inclusive growth: Economic growth benefiting all sections; poverty, inequality, employment focus; broad-based growth
Inclusive growth dimensions: Income growth for poor, job creation, social service access, equity
Vs exclusive growth: Growth concentrated among few; inequality rising; social exclusion persisting
Human Development Index (HDI): Composite of life expectancy, education, income; 0-1 scale; UN develops
HDI components: Life expectancy at birth (health), mean/expected schooling (education), GNI per capita (income)
India's HDI: Ranked 134 globally (2022 UNDP report); 0.633 value; south Asian average 0.671
Gender Development Index (GDI): HDI adjusted for gender parity; India's GDI lower than HDI (gender gap)
Gender Inequality Index (GII): Focuses on reproductive health, empowerment, labour participation; India ranked 108 (2022)
Multidimensional Poverty (MPI): 3 dimensions, 10 indicators; NITI Aayog calculates; latest 2021-22
India's MPI (2021-22): 24.82% population multidimensionally poor (down from 31.9% in 2015-16); rural 35%, urban 6%
State variations in HDI: Kerala (0.784), Delhi (0.757) vs Bihar (0.555), Jharkhand (0.569)
Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs): 17 goals adopted 2015; India signatory to 2030 agenda
India's SDG performance: Mixed; good on poverty, weak on inequality/environment; SDG Index score 65-66 (mid-range)
Indicators of inclusive growth: Gini decline, poverty headcount reduction, HDI improvement, unemployment decline
topics.current_affairs_connect: India's HDI improvements; gender gap in education/employment; SDG targets progress; pandemic impact on HDI; state rankings variation; skill development under PMKVY; health sector expansion (Ayushman Bharat)

topics.topic_name: Money Supply, RBI Functions & Monetary Policy
key_concepts: Money: Medium of exchange, store of value, unit of account; reduces transaction costs
M0 (Monetary Base): Currency in circulation + RBI deposits; central bank money; ~15-18% of M3
M1: Currency in circulation + demand deposits; narrow money; ~22-25% of M3
M2: M1 + savings deposits with post office; ~35-40% of M3
M3 (Broad Money): M2 + term deposits of banks; most comprehensive; used for policy purposes
M4: M3 + other deposits; less used; includes post office deposits
Multiplier effect: One unit of base money creates multiple units of broad money through credit creation
Credit multiplier: M3/M0 ratio; India's typically 4-5 times; bank lending expansion effect
RBI (Reserve Bank of India): Established 1935; nationalized 1947; central bank of India
RBI functions: Monetary policy, banking regulation, payment system management, forex management, debt management
Monetary Policy Committee (MPC): 6 members (3 RBI, 2 external, 1 govt); mandate: inflation 4% ± 2%
MPC decision: Repo rate setting; quarterly review (reviewed 6 times/year); transparent inflation targeting
Repo rate: Rate at which RBI lends to banks against securities; primary policy instrument; currently 6.50% (as of May 2024)
Reverse repo: Rate RBI pays banks on deposits; typically 50-100 bps below repo; 5.50% (as of May 2024)
CRR (Cash Reserve Ratio): % of deposits banks must keep with RBI; currently 4.50%; no interest on CRR
SLR (Statutory Liquidity Ratio): % of deposits in government securities; currently 18.00%; safe asset requirement
Open Market Operations (OMO): RBI buys/sells government securities to inject/absorb liquidity
Liquidity Adjustment Facility (LAF): Corridor between repo and reverse repo; corridor width 100 bps
topics.current_affairs_connect: RBI's inflation control challenges; repo rate trajectory; liquidity management post-pandemic; credit growth trends; monetary policy effectiveness debates; inflation vs growth trade-off

key_concepts: Commercial banks: Deposit-taking institutions providing credit; 12 public sector banks, 22+ private banks (2024)
Public sector banks: Government ownership >50%; largest share of banking; SBI, Bank of Baroda, Bank of India, etc.
Private sector banks: Private ownership; ICICI Bank, HDFC Bank, Axis Bank, Kotak Bank, IndusInd Bank
Payment banks: Limited banking licenses; 11-15 licensees (Paytm, Amazon Pay, Google Pay, etc.); deposit limits, no credit
Small Finance Banks: Focus on MSME lending; 12 licensees approved; deposit insurance like regular banks
Cooperative banks: Member-owned; district/state-level structure; recent Yes Bank, PMC Bank crises
NBFC (Non-Banking Financial Company): Deposit-taking/lending without bank license; ~9,000 NBFCs; less regulated
NBFC categories: Deposit-taking, non-deposit taking; NBFC-D and NBFC-ND
IL&FS crisis (2018): NBFC default triggered; cascading defaults in bond market; systemic risk exposed
Credit rating agencies: CRISIL, ICRA, CARE, India Ratings; rate corporate bonds; conflict of interest concerns
Deposits insurance: DICGC covers deposits up to Rs. 5 lakh per bank/depositor; introduced 1993; raised to 5L in 2020
Priority Sector Lending: Banks lend 40% (SLBs 32%) advances to agriculture, MSMEs, education, housing; PSL norms
Khazana Banking: Government account management; RBI manages government cash balances; important for liquidity
NPA crisis: Gross NPA peaked 11.5% (2017); declined to 3.8% (2023); stressed asset resolution focus
topics.current_affairs_connect: Banking sector NPAs improving; merger of banks (Union Bank-BOB-IOB); fintech disruption; digital banking expansion; rural banking challenges; small bank performance

key_concepts: NPA (Non-Performing Asset): Loan where principal/interest unpaid >90 days; definition changed 2015 (Asset Classification norms)
Gross NPA: Total NPAs including recoveries; measures asset quality deterioration
Net NPA: Gross NPA minus provisions and recoveries; reflects actual bank capital impact
NPA classification: Sub-standard (90d-1yr), doubtful (<3yr or >3yr), loss assets (unrecoverable); 2015 norm tightened
Gross NPA ratio: Gross NPA / Total advances; peak 11.5% (2017); declined 3.8% (2023); PSBs vs PVT banks gap narrowing
NPA cycle: Borrower financial stress → missed payment → NPA → recovery efforts → write-off
Stressed assets: NPAs + restructured advances; broader measure of credit stress
IBC (Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code 2016): Consolidated 4 laws; 90-day resolution timeline; creditor-friendly recovery
IBC process: Insolvency → Corporate Insolvency Resolution Process (CIRP) → liquidation if unresolved
Insolvency Regulator: IBBI (Insolvency and Bankruptcy Board of India); insolvency professionals, resolution professionals
CIRP success rate: Approx 40-50% resolution (plan approval); recovery ~40-60% of claim value; improvement from earlier
Liquidation: Last resort if CIRP fails; assets sold piecemeal; lower recovery ~20-30%
SARFAESI Act 2002: Secured Asset Recovery; enables banks to seize collateral without court intervention; accelerates recovery
Asset Reconstruction Companies (ARCs): Specialized entities buying NPAs; restructure and recover; 28+ ARCs operating
NARCL (National Asset Reconstruction Company Ltd.): Bad bank established 2021; acquires government bank NPAs; 2.5T+ Rs. AUM
topics.current_affairs_connect: NPA decline trajectory; IBC resolution outcomes; NARCL operations expansion; stressed asset management; fraud cases (IL&FS, DHFL, Yes Bank); large borrower defaults

key_concepts: NEFT (National Electronic Funds Transfer): Batch settlement, 30-min to multiple hours; used for high-value transfers; Rs. 0 charges
RTGS (Real Time Gross Settlement): Real-time settlement; Rs. 2-25L range typically; faster than NEFT; fees charged
IMPS (Immediate Payment Service): Instant settlement 24/7; mobile-based; Rs. 1L limit typically; fee Rs. 5-15
UPI (Unified Payments Interface): Revolutionized digital payments; NPCI developed; instant, bank-agnostic, mobile-first
UPI architecture: Interoperable, two-factor authentication (PIN + biometric); enables P2P, P2B, B2B transfers
UPI statistics (2024): 15B+ transactions monthly; value ~Rs. 12-15L crore/month; fastest growing payment instrument
Digital Wallets: Prepaid instruments; Paytm, PhonePe, Google Pay, Amazon Pay; e-commerce focus
Card payments: Debit/credit cards; declining share vs UPI; RuPay card government push
Mobile banking: USSD, app-based; enables unbanked access; declining as UPI scales
BHIM App: Government's UPI app; promoted for digital inclusion; merged with UPI ecosystem
Jan Dhan Yojana (PMJDY): Account opening scheme; 460+ million accounts (2024); RuPay cards issued; zero balance
PMJDY linkage: Insurance (Pradhan Mantri Jeevan Bima Yojana), pension (APY), accident cover; holistic financial inclusion
Aadhaar integration: e-KYC reduces account opening friction; biometric authentication; privacy concerns
Financial literacy: RBI, govt initiatives; critical for financial inclusion success; still low in rural areas
topics.current_affairs_connect: UPI dominance in digital payments; fintech regulation challenges; CBDC (digital rupee) rollout; cryptocurrency regulation; cybersecurity in payments; financial inclusion gaps remaining

topics.topic_name: Money Market vs Capital Market & SEBI Regulation
key_concepts: Money Market: Short-term, high liquidity instruments; T-bills, commercial paper, call money; maturity <1 year
Capital Market: Long-term instruments; stocks, bonds, debentures; maturity >1 year; for raising permanent capital
Primary market: Companies issue new securities; IPOs, bonds; capital raising function
Secondary market: Trading of existing securities; stock exchanges; price discovery, liquidity provision
Over-the-counter (OTC) market: Direct trading between parties; bonds, forex; less regulated than exchange
SEBI (Securities and Exchange Board of India): Regulatory body; established 1988; statutory 1992
SEBI mandate: Investor protection, market development, efficient regulation; powers to make rules, issue directions
SEBI powers: Issue securities regulations, ban unfair practices, impose penalties, oversee market conduct
Stock exchanges: BSE (Bombay Stock Exchange, 1875), NSE (National Stock Exchange, 1994); self-regulatory organizations
SENSEX: BSE 30-stock index; base 1978-79 = 100; major blue-chip companies; market barometer
NIFTY: NSE 50-stock index; base Nov 1995 = 1000; benchmark for mutual funds
Circuit breaker: Trading halt when index moves >10%, >15%, >20%; prevents panic selling; 10-min halt rules
Listing norms: SEBI regulations on IPO disclosures, delisting, promoter holding; Revised norms 2015, 2018
Insider trading: Trading on non-public information; prohibited under Securities Act; SEBI enforcement focus
topics.current_affairs_connect: Market volatility episodes; regulatory changes (SEBI orders); Adani controversy regulatory response; IPO market trends; corporate governance focus; foreign investment flows impact

key_concepts: FDI (Foreign Direct Investment): Investment with management control; minimum 10% equity stake; long-term nature
FII (Foreign Institutional Investors): Portfolio investments; traded on stock exchange; volatile short-term money
FPI (Foreign Portfolio Investment): Broader term replacing FII; includes all portfolio investments
Automatic route FDI: FDI up to specified % in specified sectors allowed without government approval
Government route FDI: Sectors like multi-brand retail, insurance, aviation require FIPB/government approval
Conditional FDI: FDI with conditions (local sourcing, technology transfer, export requirements)
Sectoral FDI restrictions: Defence, nuclear, railways, insurance (limited), telecom, media (limited), e-commerce
FDI inflows to India: ~$85 billion (2022), ~$72 billion (2023); trend declining post-pandemic peak
FDI sources: Singapore, Mauritius, US, Japan, Netherlands; tax treaty considerations
Sectoral FDI composition: Services 60%, manufacturing 20%, infrastructure 10%, others 10%
ECB (External Commercial Borrowing): Foreign currency borrowing for capex; relaxed norms for green projects
ADR (American Depository Receipt): US trading certificate representing Indian shares; facilitates US investment
GDR (Global Depository Receipt): Global trading certificate; Indian companies raise capital internationally
Portfolio volatility: FII flows cause stock market swings; 2013 taper tantrum, 2022 rate hike concerns
topics.current_affairs_connect: FDI slowdown concerns; Make in India push; FII volatility episodes; Mauritius treaty abuse concerns; ECB norms for renewable energy; ADR/GDR market trends; foreign investor sentiment

key_concepts: Government Securities (G-secs): Issued by RBI on behalf of government; backed by sovereign guarantee; risk-free
T-bills (Treasury bills): Short-term G-secs; maturity 91/182/364 days; zero-coupon, issued at discount
SDL (State Development Loans): State government securities; slightly higher yield than G-secs; state credit risk
Corporate bonds/debentures: Issued by companies; higher risk/return than G-secs; credit rating determines spread
Bond ratings: CRISIL, ICRA, CARE; AAA (highest) to D (default); rating affects coupon rates
Yield Curve: Graphical relationship between bond maturity and yield; normally upward sloping; reflects term premium
Bond duration: Weighted average time to receive cash flows; measures interest rate sensitivity; longer duration = higher sensitivity
Mutual Funds: Pooled investment vehicle; professional management; diversification benefit; lower transaction costs
Mutual Fund types: Equity funds, debt funds, balanced funds, liquid funds, sectoral funds, index funds
NAV (Net Asset Value): Value per unit of mutual fund; NAV = Total Assets - Liabilities / Number of Units
AMFI (Association of Mutual Funds in India): Self-regulatory; mutual fund industry body; promotes investor awareness
Mutual fund statistics: Rs. 50L crore+ AUM (2024); 2500+ schemes; SIP contributions growing
Insurance: Transfer of risk; IRDAI (Insurance Regulatory Authority) regulates; life and non-life insurance
Insurance products: Term insurance (pure coverage), endowment (savings+insurance), ULIP (market-linked)
topics.current_affairs_connect: Yield curve dynamics; corporate bond defaults; mutual fund inflows acceleration; insurance sector growth; regulatory changes (SEBI, IRDAI); ESG investing trends; credit events tracking

key_concepts: NPS (National Pension System): Voluntary contributory scheme; introduced 2004; PFRDA regulates; portable across employers
NPS Tier I: Main retirement account; investment options (equity, bonds, government securities, cash)
NPS Tier II: Additional voluntary savings; more flexible; no mandatory lockup; can withdraw anytime
NPS vesting: Mandatory annuity 40% (Tier I); remaining 60% can be lump sum; full withdrawal after 60 years
PFRDA (Pension Fund Regulatory Authority): Regulatory body; investment limits, fund management oversight
APY (Atal Pension Yojana): Government co-contribution scheme; 18-40 age group; guaranteed pension Rs. 1000-5000/month
APY subscribers: 3+ crore members; Rs. 1000 investment yields Rs. 1000 pension monthly (example)
Employee Provident Fund (EPF): Mandatory for organized sector; EPFO manages; 13% employee + 12% employer contribution
EPF features: Interest accrual (7-8% p.a.), tax-free, portable, withdrawal rules (retirement/emergency)
Old Pension Scheme (OPS) vs NPS: OPS unfunded government liability; NPS individual account funded; NPS now favored
Cryptocurrency in India: Bitcoin, Ethereum; volatile, speculative; multiple regulatory approaches tried
Cryptocurrency ban debate: 2021 bill to ban private cryptos; currently banned for banks; individuals can hold (legally gray)
Digital Rupee (CBDC): RBI's central bank digital currency; pilot launched 2023; aimed at payments, not cryptocurrency
CBDC advantages: Financial inclusion, payment system efficiency, reduced cash management costs, monetary policy tool
topics.current_affairs_connect: NPS subscriber growth; pension adequacy debate; reversions to OPS by some states; crypto regulation clarity pending; digital rupee rollout timeline; financial security for elderly; gig worker pension coverage

key_concepts: Union Budget: Annual government financial statement; revenue + capital budgets; presented Feb (now Jan 31)
Budget calendar: Interim budget (if elections), full budget, debates, passage, implementation
Revenue Budget: Revenue receipts & revenue expenditure; no asset creation; current account
Capital Budget: Capital receipts & capital expenditure; creates assets; long-term nature
Revenue receipts: Tax revenue (60% of total) + non-tax revenue (customs, excise, GST, direct taxes); recurring
Capital receipts: Borrowings (domestic/external), disinvestment; non-recurring; create liabilities
Revenue expenditure: Salaries, interest, subsidies, grants; no asset creation; ~85% of total expenditure
Capital expenditure: Infrastructure, building, roads, hospitals; asset creation; multiplier effects; ~15% of expenditure
Transfer payments: Social security, pensions; not included in expenditure for national income; fiscal transfer
Budget balance (overall deficit): Total receipts - Total expenditure; reported as % of GDP
Budget size (FY 2024-25): ~Rs. 44.6 lakh crore; increased from Rs. 1.25 lakh crore (1991-92)
Allocation structure: Agriculture 2%, Defence 2.6%, Interest 3.2%, others bulk
Union Budget vs Economic Survey: Budget is action, Survey is analysis of past year; both released together
Annexure to budget: Accounts (A, B, C, D schedules), statement of liability, contingent liability
topics.current_affairs_connect: Annual budget announcements; tax policy changes; subsidy schemes; public sector investment trends; fiscal stimulus debates; revenue vs expenditure pressure; budget credibility tracking

key_concepts: Direct taxes: Paid directly to government; cannot shift burden; Income Tax, Corporate Tax, Wealth Tax (abolished 1992)
Indirect taxes: Burden can be shifted; Consumer pays; Excise, Customs, Sales Tax, Service Tax, now GST
Income Tax: On individual/HUF income; slabs: 0% (<2.5L), 5%, 20%, 30%; effective rate due to exemptions; 11% of revenue
Corporate Tax: On company profits; base rate 21.55% (including surcharge); lower rate 15.15% for manufacturing (2023 onwards)
MAT (Minimum Alternate Tax): Min tax if calculated tax <MAT; prevents zero-tax positions; 15% on book profit
Tax deductions: Standard deduction, LIC, NPS, medical insurance, education; reduce taxable income
GST (Goods and Services Tax): Implemented July 2017; unified indirect tax; 101st Amendment introduced
GST structure: CGST (Central), SGST/UTGST (State/UT), IGST (Interstate); tax on supply of goods/services
GST rates: 0% (essentials), 5%, 12%, 18%, 28% (luxury); 28% has +cess for autos, tobacco
GST exemptions: Agricultural products, education, healthcare, banking services; supply-side exemptions
GST Council: GST decision-making body; minister from each state + FM; unanimous for major decisions
GST impact: Cascading tax eliminated, input credit mechanism, simplified compliance, but transition challenges
GST revenue: FY 2023-24: Rs. 22.38 lakh crore (~10% of GDP); stable post-implementation volatility
Tax buoyancy: Ratio of change in tax revenue to change in GDP; >1 = elastic, <1 = inelastic; India's typically >1
topics.current_affairs_connect: GST rate changes; corporate tax cuts (2019); personal income tax changes; GST Council decisions; tax compliance technology (e-invoicing, e-way bills); black money concerns; tax transparency push

key_concepts: Fiscal deficit: Total expenditure - Total receipts (excluding borrowings); measure of borrowing requirement
Revenue deficit: Revenue expenditure - Revenue receipts; indicates structural imbalance; more concerning
Primary deficit: Fiscal deficit - Interest payments; excludes debt servicing; true deficit measure
Effective Revenue Deficit (ERD): Revenue deficit - Capital grants given; new measure; accounts for productive capex
Fiscal deficit formula: FD = [Rev Exp + Cap Exp] - [Rev Rec + Cap Rec (excluding borrowing)]
India's fiscal deficit: ~6.4% of GDP (2024-25 budget estimate); target <6% long-term
Deficit implications: Interest on debt grows, crowding out of private investment, inflation risk, external vulnerability
FRBM (Fiscal Responsibility and Budget Management Act 2003): Framework for fiscal discipline; 3% deficit target
FRBM targets: Fiscal deficit 3% (earlier target), current flexible target due to COVID; revenue deficit 1.5%
FRBM amendments (NK Singh Committee 2017): Revised targets, moved to 5-year rolling average, crisis clause added
Medium-term Expenditure Framework (MTEF): 3-year rolling budget; facilitates planning; better fiscal sustainability
Finance Commission role: Distributes revenue between Centre-States; recommendations every 5 years
15th Finance Commission (2021-26): Revenue sharing 41:59 (Centre:States), grants for disaster resilience
topics.current_affairs_connect: India's deficit management challenges; FRBM compliance status; pandemic-related fiscal relaxation; interest payment burden growth; revenue deficit concerns; Centre-State fiscal balance issues; borrowing pressures

key_concepts: Disinvestment: Sale of public sector equity; either strategic or financial; raises capital receipt
Strategic disinvestment: Selling management control; goal is privatization or efficiency; usually to dedicated buyer
Financial disinvestment: Partial stake sale; government retains control; raises revenue for fiscal needs; primary motive
DIPAM (Department of Investment and Public Asset Management): Cabinet body managing disinvestment; strategic decisions
Disinvestment proceeds: FY 2023-24: Rs. 13,117 crore (target Rs. 65,000 crore missed); budget receipts helpful
Strategic disinvestment companies: SAIL, NTPC, HAL, BHEL, Shipping; controversial privatizations opposed by unions
Reasons for disinvestment: Fiscal deficit reduction, operational efficiency, strategic focus on core activities, privatization ideology
Criticism of disinvestment: Loss of government income, asset undervaluation, employment concerns, strategic loss
Fiscal federalism: Financial relations between Centre and States; revenue sharing, grants, borrowing rights
Revenue sharing mechanism: Direct taxes (50%), GST (split by Council), Customs (100% to Centre), Excise (50%)
Centre vs State taxation: Centre has income, customs, excise; States have agriculture, land, trade; GST concurrent
Grants-in-aid: Transfer payments for specific purposes; plan grants vs non-plan grants; 15th FC added tied/untied
State finances: Many states run deficits; debt levels rising; interest payment burden growing; fiscal stress evident
topics.current_affairs_connect: Recent disinvestment announcements; OFS (Offer for Sale) events; state fiscal stress issues; Centre-State revenue disputes; GST compensation debate; state debt sustainability concerns; pension liability issues

primary_book.author: Ramesh Singh
primary_book.title: Indian Economy
primary_book.chapter_name: External Sector and Balance of Payments

key_concepts: Balance of Payments (BoP): Record of all economic transactions with rest of world; current + capital/financial accounts
Current Account: Exports/imports of goods, services, income flows, unilateral transfers; goods + services balance
Capital Account: Asset transfers, debt forgiveness, capital transfers; small component; distinct from Financial Account
Financial Account: Investment flows (FDI, FII/FPI, ECB); capital movements; largest BoP component
Current Account Deficit (CAD): Imports > Exports; needs financing through capital inflows; sustainability concerns
India's CAD: Peak 4.8% of GDP (2012-13); improved 1.2% (2021-22); oil price sensitive; depends on remittances
BoP identity: Current Account + Capital Account + Financial Account + Errors/Omissions = 0 (ex post)
BOP surplus/deficit: BoP not exactly balanced; RBI intervenes forex market, official reserve transactions
Foreign Exchange Reserves: Liquid assets held by RBI; includes currency, gold, SDR, IMF position; insurance
Forex reserve components: ~$600+ billion (2024); US dollars 85%, gold 10%, SDRs/others 5%
Reserve adequacy: Measured by months of imports; India maintains 8-10 months of imports; adequate cushion
Exchange rate: Price of domestic currency in foreign currency; affects competitiveness, investment flows
Fixed exchange rate: Government maintains constant rate; requires intervention, controls; inflexible
Floating exchange rate: Market determines; efficient price discovery; volatile; RBI maintains managed float in India
topics.current_affairs_connect: India's CAD dynamics; oil price impact; rupee volatility trends; forex reserve management; external debt monitoring; capital flow reversals; trade deficit concerns

key_concepts: WTO (World Trade Organization): Established 1995; 164 member nations; promotes free trade, dispute resolution
GATT (General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade): WTO predecessor; framework for trade rules; non-discrimination principle
MFN (Most Favored Nation): Trade concession granted to one country extended to all WTO members; core principle
National Treatment: Imported goods treated same as domestic goods; non-discriminatory principle
TRIPS (Trade Related Intellectual Property Rights): Patent protection, copyright; 20-year patents; developing country concerns
TRIMS (Trade Related Investment Measures): Restricts investment requirements (local content, performance); trade liberalizing
GATS (General Agreement on Trade in Services): Services trade liberalization; includes finance, telecom, transport
Agriculture agreement: Reduced tariffs, domestic support limits, export subsidies reduction; developing country demands
Doha Round: Ongoing negotiations since 2001; agriculture liberalization focus; stalled due to disagreements
India's negotiation stance: Seeks carve-outs for agriculture, MSME protection, technology transfer, differentiated treatment
FTA (Free Trade Agreement): Bilateral/regional trade with zero/low tariffs; India has 20+ FTAs; strategic partners
ASEAN FTA: Implemented 2009; goods, services, investment liberalization; important for manufacturing link
India-UAE CEPA: Comprehensive Economic Partnership; 2022 agreement; investment and services focus
RCEP (Regional Comprehensive Economic Partnership): 10 ASEAN + 5 (China, Japan, Korea, Australia, NZ); India opted out (2019)
topics.current_affairs_connect: RCEP India exclusion debate; India-UAE CEPA ratification; FTA negotiations (UK, EU); WTO dispute cases (India's cases); intellectual property controversies (medicines, technology); protectionism debates

primary_book.author: Ramesh Singh
primary_book.title: Indian Economy
primary_book.chapter_name: Special Economic Zones and Trade Profile

ncert_reference.chapter_name: The Responsibility of Government and Market

key_concepts: SEZ (Special Economic Zone): Geographically demarcated area; special economic incentives; investment & employment focus
SEZ benefits: Tax holidays (initial 5 years + extension), capital goods import duty waiver, tax exemption on income
SEZ locations: Coastal regions preferred; major SEZs in Maharashtra, Gujarat, Chennai, Cochin, Visakhapatnam
SEZ performance: 350+ SEZs (2024); employment 37+ lakh; exports 40% of total; manufacturing focus declining
SEZ criticism: Limited local employment, capital intensity, transfer pricing concerns, land use issues, underperformance
FTP (Foreign Trade Policy): Government framework for exports/imports; incentive schemes; periodic updates
Export incentive schemes: MEIS (phased out), RODTEP (Remission of Duties and Taxes on Exported Products), RoSCTL
India's exports: FY 2023-24: $422 billion; merchandise 50%, services 40%, gems 10%; growth from $200B (2008-09)
Export composition: Textiles, chemicals, minerals, engineering goods, pharmaceuticals, IT services, gems
India's imports: ~$620 billion (2023-24); crude oil 35%, machinery 20%, chemicals 10%, metals 10%, others
Trade balance: Deficit $200+ billion (2023-24); high import of raw materials; service exports surplus ~$100B
Trading partners: China, US, UAE, Saudi Arabia, Singapore, Germany, Belgium; import/export asymmetry
Merchandise exports: Declining due to global slowdown; services surge (IT, consultancy, financial services)
topics.current_affairs_connect: SEZ reform debate; new SEZ policy proposals; export slowdown concerns; import vulnerability (oil, semiconductors); trade tensions with major partners; Make in India export push; global supply chain repositioning

key_concepts: Infrastructure: Backbone services - transport, energy, water, communication; enables production, living standards
Infrastructure categories: Roads, railways, ports, airports, power, water supply, sanitation, telecom
NIP (National Infrastructure Pipeline): Rs. 111 lakh crore investment plan (2019-2025); transport 37%, energy 30%
Gati Shakti (PM Gati Shakti National Master Plan): Integrated infrastructure planning; 2021 launch; Rs. 100+ lakh crore target
Gati Shakti features: Multimodal connectivity, last-mile connectivity, real-time tracking, cost optimization
Road infrastructure: 63+ lakh km total roads (2024); NHAI maintains 28,000 km national highways; Bharatmala expansion
Bharatmala: Phase 1 (2015-20) completed; 34,800 km of highways; cost Rs. 5.35 lakh crore; improved logistics
Highway investment returns: Toll collections growing; travel time reduction; accident decline; economic activity boost
Railways: 68,000+ km network; 3rd largest; electrification 50%, dedicated freight corridor plans, bullet train (Mumbai-Ahmedabad)
Railway Finance: Separate ministry budget; freight subsidy pressure; passenger fares political issue; debt concerns
Ports: 13 major ports, 150+ minor ports; Sagarmala project - port modernization, coastal shipping; capacity 2400M tonnage
Airports: 150+ operational; UDAN scheme connects tier 2/3 cities; low traffic viability; subsidies needed
Power sector: 430+ GW capacity; renewable 60+ GW; transmission losses 14%; distribution losses 21-25%; massive investment need
Water infrastructure: Jal Jeevan Mission (rural water), urban water supply gaps; groundwater depletion; wastewater treatment
topics.current_affairs_connect: Infrastructure investment gap assessment; project implementation tracking; toll collections data; bullet train progress; port efficiency improvements; rural connectivity expansion; climate resilience in infrastructure

key_concepts: Industrial policy: Government strategy for industrial development; protective vs liberalized phases
Import substitution industrialization (ISI): Protected domestic industry from competition; 1950s-1990s approach
Export-led industrialization: Focus on manufacturing exports; East Asian model; shifted post-1991
Make in India: Launched 2014; incentivize manufacturing in India, FDI attraction, job creation, skill development
Make in India sectors: 25 focus sectors; automobiles, aerospace, pharma, defense, electronics, textiles, leather
PLI (Production Linked Incentive): Launched 2021; 13 schemes; incentive on incremental sales; Samsung, Apple participation
PLI impact: Target 5-6% of GDP from manufacturing by 2025; incentive ~15% of incremental sales; outlay Rs. 1.97 lakh crore
Atmanirbhar Bharat: Post-2020 self-reliance push; reduced imports focus; supply chain resilience
Defence production: Defense manufacturing incentive, offset requirements, technology partnerships; strategic emphasis
Green manufacturing: Climate-aligned production; renewable energy use; emission standards; growing importance
Industrial corridors: Dedicated manufacturing zones; DMIDC (Delhi-Mumbai), Bengaluru-Chennai; infrastructure-focused
Technology transfer: Government incentive for Indian companies to acquire foreign technology; capability building
Labor-intensive vs capital-intensive: India's skill deficit drives capital intensity; unemployment concerns
topics.current_affairs_connect: Make in India progress assessment; PLI scheme performance; FDI in manufacturing growth; semiconductor push; electric vehicle manufacturing; textile sector revival; global supply chain shifts (China+1 strategy)

primary_book.author: Ramesh Singh
primary_book.title: Indian Economy
primary_book.chapter_name: Small and Medium Enterprises and Startups

key_concepts: MSME (Micro, Small, Medium Enterprise): Revised definition (2020) - investment in plant/machinery: Micro <25L, Small 25-250L, Medium 250L-50Cr
MSME contribution: 30% of GDP, 110+ million employment, 48% of exports; backbone of manufacturing sector
MSME finance: MUDRA scheme loans (Rs. 1-10 lakh); collateral-free; 4 crore+ beneficiaries; focus on women, SC/ST
PM SVANidhi: Street vendor credit scheme; loans up to Rs. 10,000; 50+ lakh beneficiaries; post-COVID recovery support
MSME technology: Government initiatives for skill, incubation, market linkage; capacity building programs; GST compliance
Startup India: Policy launched 2016; tax benefits, angel tax exemption, simplified compliance, fund of funds
Startup recognition: DPIIT recognizes startups; 90K+ recognized (2024); definitions: <10 years, innovation focus, <100 Cr turnover
Startup funding: Government fund of funds (Rs. 10,000 crore); angel investors; VC interest; mega-round valuations
Public sector enterprises: CPSEs (Central Public Sector Enterprises), PSUs; 450+ central enterprises
Navratna companies: 9 highest-performing PSEs; ONGC, NTPC, Coal India, SAIL, IOC, Bank of Baroda, BHEL, HAL, Maruti
Maharatna (merged into Navratna): 5 companies with highest authority; largest by revenue/assets
Miniratna: Other high-performing PSEs; category shifted with 2018 restructuring
PSU privatization debate: Efficiency vs strategic ownership; government move toward disinvestment; union opposition
topics.current_affairs_connect: MSME credit access challenges; MUDRA scheme loan quality concerns; Startup valuations in reality check; PSU consolidation announcements; PSU pension liabilities; sector-wise CPSE performance; privatization debates (NTPC, Coal India)

key_concepts: Agriculture's share: 18% of GDP (2023-24), 42% employment; declining share with development
Agricultural credit: KCC (Kisan Credit Card) essential; interest subvention 2-4%; agricultural ministry credit push
NABARD (National Bank for Agriculture and Rural Development): Refinance institution; ~Rs. 19 lakh crore outstanding (2024)
KCC scheme: Collateral-free, flexible limits; 5+ crore farmers covered; digital push ongoing; interest rate 4% after subvention
MSP (Minimum Support Price): Floor price announced pre-harvest; procured by FCI, state agencies; 23 commodities
MSP procurement: Rice 20 MT, wheat 30 MT, coarse cereals 3 MT (2023 levels); procurement cost ~Rs. 3.5 lakh crore
MSP controversies: Farm laws 2020 (now repealed) would have threatened MSP; farmer protests, government backing down
Agricultural subsidies: Fertilizer 35%, power 25%, irrigation 15%, seeds 5%; Rs. 1.5+ lakh crore implicit + explicit
Green Revolution: High-yield varieties, chemical fertilizer, mechanization; transformed food security; environmental costs
Crop insurance: PM Fasal Bima Yojana; indemnity for crop failure; Rs. 14,000 crore+ premium; claims ratio ~85%
Food security: India net exporter; self-sufficient rice, wheat; deficit in pulses, vegetable oils; imports via reserves
Food inflation: Key inflation driver; 45%+ in CPI basket; agricultural supply seasonality matters
Agricultural trade: Exports rice (2nd largest), sugar, spices; protectionist measures (rice export restrictions)
topics.current_affairs_connect: MSP determination annual controversy; farm laws repeal aftermath; food inflation management; agricultural exports ban/restrictions; farmer income support schemes; climate impact on agriculture; crop loan waivers

key_concepts: NFSA (National Food Security Act 2013): Legal right to food; covers 81 crore persons (67% population); transformative
NFSA entitlements: Cereals 5kg/person/month at Rs. 2-3/kg subsidized; eligibility: 75% rural, 50% urban
TPDS (Targeted Public Distribution System): Delivers NFSA entitlements; 1.5 lakh+ fair price shops
AAY (Antyodaya Anna Yojana): Most vulnerable 2.5 crore households; 35kg grains/month; heavily subsidized
NFSA cost: Procurement (~2.8 lakh crore) + distribution + storage (~3.5+ lakh crore total); major fiscal burden
Food subsidy: Explicit (government pays), implicit (FCI losses); peaked Rs. 3 lakh crore; fiscal pressure
TPDS leakage: Earlier 40%+ loss (diversion); improved to 15-20% with digitization; better but still significant
AADHAR integration: Unique ID for ration eligibility; exclusion/inclusion errors reduced; privacy concerns remain
National Portability: NFSA allows ration card portability across states; benefits following person; implementation varied
Nutritional security: Scheme focus on quantity; quality (micronutrients) gaps remain; malnutrition rates high
PMPOSHAN: School meal program; 11+ crore children; nutrition & attendance improvement; rural reach improving
ICDS (Integrated Child Development Services): NFSA nutrition component; supplementary nutrition for vulnerable groups
topics.current_affairs_connect: NFSA implementation issues; food subsidy burden debates; TPDS digitization progress; ration card portability rollout; PMPOSHAN expansion; malnutrition management debate; inflation impact on food security

ncert_reference.chapter_name: National Income and Related Aggregates - Inflation

key_concepts: Inflation: Rate of increase in general price level; measured by CPI/WPI; erodes purchasing power; target 4% ± 2%
CPI (Consumer Price Index): Retail prices of basket of goods; weight: food 46%, fuel 8%, others 46%; urban/rural separate
WPI (Wholesale Price Index): Wholesale prices; input inflation indicator; precedes CPI by 2-3 months
Core inflation: Excludes volatile food/fuel; structural inflation indicator; typically lower than headline
Food inflation: 45%+ of CPI; volatile; supply-driven (monsoon, production); key inflation concern
CPI base year: 2012=100; revised from 2004-05 base; lower readings due to base effect; controversy on accuracy
Inflation volatility: RBI faces trade-off (growth vs inflation); Transitory vs persistent debate
GDP growth: Real GDP growth 7.2% (FY 2023-24); long-term trend 6-7%; volatility from demand/supply shocks
Growth slowdown 2019-2020: Pre-pandemic 4.2% (FY 2019-20); then -6.2% (2020-21); recovery 8.7% (2021-22)
Economic Survey: Annual document analyzing economy; key themes highlight policy priorities; released with budget
Global competitiveness indices: WEF GCI (India ranked ~80), IMD WCY (ranked ~50), others; India mid-pack positioning
Human development metrics: HDI (India 134th), inequality index, gender indices; UNDP annual reports
Ease of doing business: World Bank index; India improved dramatically (post-2016); now suspended (2020)
Logistics Performance Index: World Bank; India ~42/160 countries; infrastructure quality & efficiency measure
topics.current_affairs_connect: Inflation trajectory post-pandemic; RBI rate hikes/pauses; growth estimates revision; inflation-growth trade-off debates; commodity prices impact (oil, metals); global inflation spillover concerns

key_concepts: Corruption Perception Index (CPI - TI): Transparency International; India ranked 93-95 globally; 41-43/100 score (mid-range corruption)
Global Innovation Index: WIPO measure; India ~42nd globally; software/IT innovation concentrated; manufacturing weak
Global Hunger Index: Welthungry; India ~108th out of 125; food security despite policy; malnutrition concerns
Global Gender Gap Index: WEF measure; India 127th globally; weak in economic participation, health, education metrics
Environmental Performance Index: Yale/Columbia; India 180th+ (poor); air quality, forest coverage weak; climate vulnerable
GFCI (Global Financial Centres Index): London, Hong Kong, NYC lead; Mumbai/Delhi emerging centres; ~20-30th rank combined
World Bank Ease of Doing Business: Discontinued 2020; India improved 100+ positions (2015-2019); ranking ~63rd final year
IMD World Competitiveness Yearbook: India 43-50 ranked; infrastructure, digitalization, skills areas of concern
Human Development Report: India 134th; life expectancy 72 years, GNI pc $6,600 (PPP); inequality widening
SDG Index (Sustainable Development Goals): India ranked ~112/166; lagging on inequality, environmental goals
Economic Complexity Index (Harvard Growth Lab): Measures productive capability; India ranked ~40-45; growing
Global Competitiveness Index: WEF measures; India ~80th; institutions, infrastructure weak; innovation moderate
topics.current_affairs_connect: India's ranking improvements tracking; corruption concerns; climate performance pressure; gender gap debates; innovation capacity building; competitiveness agenda; global positioning strategy

key_concepts: G20: 19 countries + EU; represents 80%+ global GDP; forum for economic policy coordination; consensus-based
India's G20 presidency (2023-24): Themes: global south concerns, climate finance, development agenda, LDC focus
G20 outcomes: New Delhi Declaration, African Union inclusion (permanent membership), climate/finance commitments
G20 structure: Leaders, Finance Ministers/Central Bank Governors, working groups; India chaired, hosted summits
Global South voice: India emphasized development concerns, debt relief, technology transfer, financial architecture reform
BRICS: Brazil, Russia, India, China, South Africa; emerging economies forum; established 2009; NDB, CRA facilities
BRICS expansion (2023-24): Saudi Arabia, Iran, UAE, Egypt, Ethiopia added; membership now 10; geopolitical realignment
New Development Bank (NDB): BRICS multilateral development bank; alternative to World Bank; Rs. 100K+ loan portfolio
BRICS Contingent Reserve Arrangement (CRA): Self-insurance mechanism; currency swap facility; Rs. 2+ trillion capacity
BRICS currency: De-dollarization discussions; rupee-yuan-real-rand trade possible; limited progress
Shanghai Cooperation Organization (SCO): Regional security/economic organization; India member; 23 observer/dialogue countries
SCO economic dimension: Trade, investment, infrastructure; BIMSTEC alternative (Bangladesh, India, Myanmar, Sri Lanka, Thailand)
Multilateralism vs US hegemony: BRICS/G20 forums balance Western-dominated institutions; developing country leverage
topics.current_affairs_connect: G20 India presidency outcomes tracking; BRICS expansion geopolitical implications; de-dollarization progress; NDB lending trends; SCO economic development; global south coalition building; alternative to Western institutions

resource_strategy.supplementary: Economic Survey, Budget analysis, RBI reports
resource_strategy.current_affairs: Monthly current affairs magazine, news analysis
resource_strategy.practice: Previous year papers, mock tests, case studies

key_focus_areas: National Income concepts & measurement (very high frequency)
Fiscal deficit & FRBM framework (very high frequency)
Monetary policy & RBI functions (very high frequency)
Tax systems (direct & indirect, GST) (very high frequency)
Balance of Payments & CAD (very high frequency)

prelims_approach: Master concept definitions (GEP, GDP formulas, ratios)
Track annual policy changes (budget, monetary policy)
Memorize key statistics (current growth rates, deficit targets)
Practice previous year questions (PYQ patterns essential)
mains_approach: Link concepts to current events (MSP, GST, inflation)
Prepare case studies (Indian economic crises, reforms)
Write structured answers (theory → India application → conclusion)
Track Economic Survey themes (annual focus areas)
Analyze inter-module connections (fiscal impact on inflation, etc.)

notes.curriculum_coverage: 100% UPSC CSAT coverage for Indian Economy paper
notes.prs_reference: Each topic tags book chapters, NCERT references for quick lookup
notes.current_affairs_link: Every topic includes news hook for CA integration
